query: React keys in lists interview question

competitors.label: Rendering Lists
competitors.selectionReason: This is the official React page for list rendering and keys, so it is the canonical baseline.
competitors.winReason: React docs teach keys well, but our page is more explicit about production identity bugs like checked rows jumping, wrong input state sticking, and why keys are a debugging tool rather than just a performance hint.
theirStrengths: It is the official reference for how and why to supply keys in lists.
theirGaps: It is lighter on list-state corruption examples and interview-style bug framing.

realWorldUseCases: Keys are not just a performance hint. In React they define item identity, which is why a bad key can create production bugs: checked boxes jump rows, inputs keep the wrong text after reordering, or a row preserves state that belonged to a different item.
followUpConfusion: Using array indices as keys works for static lists but can cause issues in lists that reorder or update dynamically.

realWorldUseCases: The docs explain that keys identify list items, but they are less vivid about reordered-row state leakage and checkbox/input bugs.
actionableExamples: They include concrete list examples with ids, so example quality stays close.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more direct about why index keys break identity when rows reorder and why random keys are not safe.

actionableExamples: Add one before-and-after example that reorders rows and shows index keys corrupting input state.

competitors.label: React Interview Questions Quiz
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd's React question bank is directly relevant because it covers common React interview topics like reconciliation, lists, and rendering behavior.
competitors.winReason: The question bank is broad and useful, but our page is more focused on the exact list-identity bugs interviewers usually want: reordered rows, state leakage, and why keys are not just a generic optimization hint.
theirStrengths: It is broad, interview-focused, and covers many React rendering topics in one place.
theirGaps: It is less focused on one concept than a dedicated keys page.

realWorldUseCases: Strong answers frame keys as a debugging tool for list correctness, not only as reconciliation trivia.
followUpConfusion: Do not use random or non-deterministic values as keys — React relies on key consistency across renders.

realWorldUseCases: The question bank covers React rendering topics broadly, but it is less concentrated on production list-identity failures.
actionableExamples: Its quiz format still gives focused examples and solutions, so the example criterion stays close.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more explicit about index keys, state preservation, and why bad keys attach the wrong state to the wrong row.

actionableExamples: Add a short example with controlled inputs inside a sortable list.

competitors.label: React key attribute
competitors.selectionReason: DeveloperWay has one of the strongest deep dives on key semantics, random keys, index keys, and reconciliation outcomes.
theirStrengths: It is deeply focused on how keys affect reconciliation, remounting, and render behavior.
theirGaps: It is longer and more tutorial-like than a short interview answer.

realWorldUseCases: Random keys do not just rerender rows. They remount them
actionableExamples: Index keys break when rows reorder; typed text and checked state can move to the wrong todo

realWorldUseCases: The article explains real consequences of missing, random, and index keys, so the real-world lens is close.
actionableExamples: It contains more worked examples around random keys, sorting, and remount behavior than our current page.
followUpConfusion: Both pages explain that keys are about identity, not only about suppressing warnings.

realWorldUseCases: Add one explicit example of random keys forcing remounts and losing local state.
actionableExamples: Add a concrete sortable-list example with stable ids versus index keys.
followUpConfusion: Keep one extra note on the narrow static-list case where index keys are still acceptable.

competitors.winReason: Our page now makes the random-key remount bug and the sortable-list index-key failure explicit, so it is stronger on real-world and actionable content while the index-key nuance remains close.
